steps.title: Saying Hello 👋
steps.desc: In this lesson, you'll learn how Dutch people greet each other at different times of day. Dutch greetings are very logical — they literally describe the time!

steps.note: The universal Dutch greeting. Works anytime, anywhere, with anyone. You can't go wrong with 'hallo'!
Used from sunrise until about noon. 'Goede' means 'good' and 'morgen' means 'morning'. See the logic? 😊
Used from roughly noon to 6 PM. 'Middag' = afternoon (literally 'mid-day'). A bit formal — you'd use this in shops or with strangers.
Used from about 6 PM onwards. 'Avond' = evening. Notice the pattern: goede + time of day!

steps.title: 🧠 See the pattern?
steps.text:
All formal Dutch greetings follow the same formula:

Goede + time of day

• goede + morgen = goedemorgen
• goede + middag = goedemiddag
• goede + avond = goedenavond

This makes them easy to remember!

steps.q: How do you say 'good morning' in Dutch?
It's 3 PM. Which greeting fits best?

steps.note: Casual and friendly — used constantly in daily Dutch life. You'll hear it everywhere: at shops, on the street, between neighbours. In the Netherlands, even strangers greet each other with 'hoi'. It's warm but informal.

steps.example: Hey! Wat leuk je te zien!
steps.exampleEn: Hey! How nice to see you!
steps.note: Even more casual than 'hoi'. Used a lot in text messages and between close friends.

steps.title: 👔 Formal or casual?
steps.text:
Dutch people switch between formal and casual greetings:

🏢 Formal (shops, strangers, older people):
Goedemorgen / Goedemiddag / Goedenavond

😊 Casual (friends, family, peers):
Hallo / Hoi / Hey

When in doubt, start formal — the other person will usually say 'je mag jij zeggen' (you can use informal) if they prefer casual.

deepDive.title: Why does Dutch have formal and informal?
deepDive.text:
English used to have this too! 'Thou' was informal and 'you' was formal. Over time, English dropped 'thou' and kept only 'you'.

Dutch kept both:
• jij/je = informal 'you' (like old English 'thou')
• u = formal 'you'

The Netherlands is actually quite informal compared to Germany or France. Many Dutch people quickly switch to 'je'. But it's respectful to start with 'u' when meeting someone older or in a professional context.

Fun fact: In Belgium, people use 'u' much more than in the Netherlands!

steps.q: You walk into a bakery at 9 AM. You say:
steps.hint: Which greeting matches the time of day?

steps.title: Saying Goodbye ✌️
steps.desc: Now that you can say hello, let's learn how to leave! Dutch has different goodbyes for different situations.

steps.note: 'Dag' is a Swiss army knife — it works as both hello AND goodbye! Slightly formal. Often repeated for warmth: 'dag dag!'
The formal goodbye. 'Tot' means 'until' and 'ziens' means 'seeing'. So literally: 'until seeing (again)'. Use in professional settings.
Very casual and friendly — like 'bye-bye!' in English. You'll hear Dutch kids say this a LOT. Perfectly fine for adults with friends too.

steps.title: 💡 'Tot' = 'Until / See you...'
steps.text:
Dutch uses 'tot' (until) + time to say when you'll meet again:

• tot morgen = see you tomorrow
• tot later = see you later
• tot zo = see you soon
• tot volgende week = see you next week

These are incredibly common and useful!

steps.note: 'Tot' = until, 'morgen' = tomorrow. Dutch people use these 'tot' combinations all the time.

steps.exampleEn: I have to go. See you later!
steps.note: Very common in everyday speech. Works in both formal and informal situations.

steps.q: Leaving a formal business meeting — what do you say?

steps.title: Please & Thank You 🙏
steps.desc: Politeness goes a long way in the Netherlands! Let's learn the essential polite words.

steps.example: Dank je wel voor je hulp!
steps.note: The standard 'thank you' for informal situations. 'Dank' = thanks, 'je' = you (informal), 'wel' adds emphasis. You can also just say 'dank je' or even 'bedankt' (thanks!).
Same as above but with 'u' (formal you) instead of 'je'. Use in shops, restaurants, with older people, or anyone you don't know well.

steps.title: 🔑 The je/u secret
steps.text:
This is one of the most important patterns in Dutch:

• 'je' version = informal (friends, peers)
• 'u' version = formal (strangers, elders, professional)

This applies to many phrases:
• dank je / dank u
• alsjeblieft / alstublieft
• hoe heet je / hoe heet u

Spot the pattern? 'je' ↔ 'u' is the switch!

deepDive.title: How 'alsjeblieft' is actually a full sentence
deepDive.text:
'Alsjeblieft' looks like one word but it's actually a compressed sentence!

als + je + blieft = 'if it pleases you'

Breaking it down:
• als = if
• je = you (informal)
• belieft = pleases (old Dutch verb)

So when you say 'alsjeblieft', you're literally saying 'if it pleases you' — very polite!

The formal version 'alstublieft' works the same way:
als + t + u + belieft = 'if it pleases you' (formal)

The 't' comes from the formal verb conjugation. Most Dutch people don't even realize this — you'll impress them if you know it!

steps.note: Two meanings! (1) 'please' when asking for something, and (2) 'here you go' when handing something to someone. Context makes it clear.
The formal version. Notice 'je' → 'u' again! A waiter would say this when bringing your order.

steps.q: How do you say 'please' to a friend?

steps.note: Same word as English! Used when you've made a mistake or bump into someone. Dutch people say it a lot.
Used to get someone's attention or ask to pass. More polite than 'sorry'. You'll use this on crowded trains!

steps.q: Someone bumps into you on the street. They say:

steps.title: What's Your Name? 🤝
steps.desc: Time to introduce yourself! This is your first real Dutch conversation pattern.

steps.note: Literally means 'I am called...' — 'ik' = I, 'heet' = am called. This is THE way to introduce yourself in Dutch.
Literally: 'How are you called?' Use this with people your own age, in casual situations.
The formal version — remember the je/u switch! Use with older people, in professional settings, or when unsure.

steps.title: 🗣️ Your first conversation!
steps.text:
Here's a typical Dutch introduction:

A: Hallo! Ik heet Anna. Hoe heet jij?
B: Hoi! Ik heet Mark. Aangenaam!
A: Aangenaam!

That's it — you just had a conversation! 🎉

'Aangenaam' (nice to meet you) is the polite way to close an introduction. You can also say 'leuk je te ontmoeten' (nice to meet you) but 'aangenaam' is simpler.

steps.exampleEn: Nice to meet you! I'm Lisa.
steps.note: A polite way to say 'pleased to meet you.' Often accompanied by a handshake in professional settings.
Another way to introduce yourself: 'Ik ben [name]'. 'Ben' is the 'I' form of 'zijn' (to be). You'll use this verb constantly!

steps.q: How to ask someone's name informally?
Meeting your friend's grandfather — ask his name:

steps.title: How Are You? 💬
steps.desc: The final piece of basic conversation — asking how someone is and responding. After this, you can have a real mini-conversation!
goals: Put it ALL together in a conversation

steps.en: How are you? / How's it going?
steps.example: Hoi! Hoe gaat het met je?
steps.note: The standard 'how are you'. Literally: 'How goes it?' You can add 'met je' (with you, informal) or 'met u' (formal) at the end.
The most common response. Note the pronunciation — 'goed' sounds like 'hoot' (the 'g' is a throat sound unique to Dutch!).
A step above 'goed' — means everything is great. Very common and natural-sounding.
The natural follow-up after answering. 'Jou' is the informal 'you' (object form). For formal: 'En met u?'

steps.title: 🎬 A complete Dutch conversation!
steps.text:
You now know enough for a full greeting:

A: Goedemorgen! Hoe heet je?
B: Ik heet Sophie. En jij?
A: Ik heet Jan. Aangenaam!
B: Aangenaam! Hoe gaat het?
A: Goed, dank je! En met jou?
B: Prima! Tot ziens!
A: Dag!

🎉 That's a REAL Dutch conversation! You could actually do this in the Netherlands right now!

deepDive.title: The Dutch 'g' sound — the hardest part
deepDive.text:
You may have noticed that Dutch has a unique throat sound for the letter 'g'. It's the most distinctive feature of Dutch pronunciation!

The 'g' in 'goedemorgen', 'goed', and 'gaat' is NOT like the English 'g'. It's a friction sound made in the back of your throat — similar to gargling or the 'ch' in Scottish 'loch'.

Tips to master it:
• Start by saying 'aaah' like at the doctor
• Now try to make that same sound but with friction
• Practice with: goed, gaan, groot, groen

Fun fact: In the south of the Netherlands and in Belgium, they use a softer 'g' — almost like a gentle 'h'. Both versions are correct!

steps.q: Someone asks 'Hoe gaat het?' — you reply:

steps.hint: Mirror it back — like 'nice to meet you too'

steps.q: 8 AM, entering a shop. The shopkeeper is older. You say:
